one_line_takeaway: By aggregating cognitive-hacking, instruction-injection, and few-shot-hacking tricks into a single developer-role prompt template and resolving user/developer template semantic inconsistency via Adversarial Context Alignment, DH-CoT jailbreaks commercial reasoning LLMs (including GPT-5, Gemini-2.5-Flash-Thinking, DeepSeek-R1) at ASRs up to 1.00 and transfers from the developer role to the system role, while a companion LLM+human detection framework (MDH) is used to build a cleaner 1,155-prompt red-teaming benchmark (RTA) by filtering out benign and non-obviously-harmful prompts that were inflating baseline ASR in prior datasets.
key_finding: On the 8-model reasoning-LLM benchmark (Table 4, MaliciousEducator), CoT-based black-box attacks dominate: DH-CoT-D12 (developer role) reaches ASR up to 0.90 on GPT-5, 0.92 on GPT-5.1, 0.40 on o3, 0.58 on o4-Mini, 1.00 on Gemini-2.5-Flash-Thinking, 0.82 on Claude-3-7-Sonnet-Thinking, 0.20 on Claude-Sonnet-4-Thinking, and 1.00 on DeepSeek-R1 -- outperforming H-CoT on every model (H-CoT: 0.54/0.84/0.20/0.40/0.98/0.14/0.08/0.56 respectively) and far outperforming template-based black-box baselines (TAP, DeepInception, SelfCipher, D-Attack), which mostly scored 0 on the reasoning models GPT-5 through o4-Mini and only succeeded intermittently on Gemini-2.5-Flash-Thinking/DeepSeek-R1. DH-CoT's developer-role template also transfers to the system role with comparable ASR on most models except GPT-5, where the system role notably outperforms the developer role. The ablation (Table 5, o3/o4-Mini) shows: H-CoT baseline alone gets 0.20/0.40 ASR; naive aggregation of COG+INSTR+FSH without ACA gets 0.23/0.30 (no meaningful gain); adding ACA on top of the aggregated template lifts ASR to 0.28/0.47; and swapping FSH few-shot examples from explicitly harmful (EHP, 0.31/0.51) to non-obvious harmful (NHP, 0.33/0.55) to non-triggering (NTP/NFH, 0.39/0.58) monotonically improves ASR, i.e., weaker-looking few-shot examples jailbreak reasoning models better than explicitly malicious ones. On the detection side, MDH achieves >95% successful-detection rate with <10% human review both for NHP data-cleaning (Table 16) and jailbreak-response labeling (Table 17). After MDH-based cleaning, prompt complemented rejection rate (CRR) on RTA-* datasets drops sharply from the uncleaned originals (e.g., SafeBench CRR 0.44 on GPT-4o vs. RTA-SafeBench 0.17; JailbreakStudy 0.40 vs. RTA-JailbreakStudy 0.14 on GPT-4o), and for stronger models (GPT-4.1, o3, o4-Mini) CRR falls to 2-5% once Adult Content prompts are excluded, which the authors present as evidence RTA is a cleaner, higher-toxicity benchmark for measuring genuine attack gains rather than dataset artifacts.
core_contribution: The paper presents two artifacts. (1) DH-CoT, a black-box, template-based jailbreak attack against commercial reasoning LLMs that aggregates three prior jailbreak techniques (Cognitive Hacking, Direct Instruction, Few-Shot Hacking) into a single 'developer role' prompt template via five steps: Template Structure Imitation (decomposing the official benign developer template into Role Definition / Behavior Constraint / Few-Shot Context Learning), Role Definition (COG-based malicious framing), Behavior Constraint (INSTR-based refusal suppression), Few-Shot Context Learning using 'NTP-based Few-shot Hacking' (NFH, few-shot examples drawn from Non-Triggering harmful-response Prompts rather than explicitly harmful ones), and Adversarial Context Alignment (ACA), which the authors identify as the key fix that resolves semantic inconsistency between the user template (a fabricated chain-of-thought adapted from H-CoT) and the malicious developer template. The simpler four-step variant without the CoT user template is called D-Attack. (2) MDH, a malicious-content detection framework combining LLM-based judger selection, type-based pre-filtering, a three-round multi-judger voting scheme with dynamic per-judger thresholds, and human review (~10 samples per annotation) for ambiguous cases. MDH is used both to clean red-teaming benchmark datasets (removing Benign Prompts and Non-obvious Harmful Prompts) and to score jailbreak responses for Attack Success Rate (ASR) computation. Applying MDH to five existing datasets (SafeBench, QuestionSet, JailbreakStudy, BeaverTails, MaliciousEducator) yields the RTA suite: 1,155 prompts across 14 malicious categories, all classified as Explicitly Harmful Prompts (EHPs).
threat_model: Stated explicitly in Appendix B.1. Attacker goal: bypass safety alignment of commercial black-box LLMs to elicit outputs fulfilling illegitimate objectives (hate speech, violence facilitation, illegal-activity advice). Attacker capability class: black-box -- the attacker has only API-level query access (no model weights/gradients as in white-box, and no iterative multi-query optimization loop against the victim as in gray-box methods like PAIR/TAP), and constructs a payload-agnostic template that transfers across queries and models. The paper specifically targets the newly introduced 'developer' role (OpenAI o-series/GPT-5) alongside the 'system' role, arguing that multi-role prompting expands the attack surface; DH-CoT is built for the developer role but the paper explicitly tests and reports its transfer to the system role.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (paper's Limitations section): (1) MDH still requires manual review for a small fraction of hard-to-classify samples, and cumulative human effort could become non-negligible at large scale; this can be mitigated by threshold/judger-count tuning but is not eliminated. (2) Judger models vary in sensitivity to prompt types, so MDH relies on judger-specific prompts that would require re-tuning when new judger models are introduced, adding overhead. (3) For DH-CoT, while the developer-role template itself transfers across data samples without modification, the H-CoT component embedded in the user template requires minor per-sample adjustment (though this is described as fully automated and low-overhead). Additional caveats visible in the results but not framed as 'limitations' by the authors: DH-CoT's ASR against o3 tops out at 0.40 and against Claude-Sonnet-4-Thinking at only 0.20, i.e., the attack is far from universally effective on the most defended reasoning models; ASR figures are computed via the paper's own MDH judge rather than fully independent third-party labeling for the main attack table, so detector-attack circularity (the same team built both) is a validity consideration the paper does not explicitly discuss as a threat to internal validity.

datasets_used: SafeBench (Gong et al. 2025) -- source dataset, cleaned into RTA-SafeBench
QuestionSet (Shen et al. 2024) -- source dataset, cleaned into RTA-QuestionSet
JailbreakStudy (Liu et al. 2023) -- source dataset, cleaned into RTA-JailbreakStudy
BeaverTails (Ji et al. 2023) -- source dataset, cleaned into RTA-BeaverTails; only dataset containing both NHPs and NTPs
MaliciousEducator (Kuo et al. 2025) -- source dataset, already high quality, only format-standardized, used as the primary ASR evaluation set in Table 4/5
RTA suite (paper's own construction) -- 1,155 explicitly-harmful prompts across 14 malicious categories, spanning the five cleaned datasets above
ResponsesTest -- a 20-sample mixed benign/malicious response set built by the authors to validate MDH's response-detection accuracy
quantitative_results: DH-CoT-D12 (developer role) ASR on MaliciousEducator: GPT-5 0.90, GPT-5.1 0.92, o3 0.40, o4-Mini 0.58, Gemini-2.5-Flash-Thinking 1.00, Claude-3-7-Sonnet-Thinking 0.82, Claude-Sonnet-4-Thinking 0.20, DeepSeek-R1 1.00 (Table 4)
H-CoT baseline ASR on same models: 0.54, 0.84, 0.20, 0.40, 0.98, 0.14, 0.08, 0.56 (Table 4)

Template-based black-box baselines (TAP/DeepInception/SelfCipher/D-Attack) mostly score 0 ASR on GPT-5 through o4-Mini, succeeding intermittently only on Gemini-2.5-Flash-Thinking and DeepSeek-R1 (Table 4)

Ablation on o3/o4-Mini (Table 5): H-CoT baseline 0.20/0.40 -> naive TSI aggregation 0.23/0.30 -> + ACA 0.28/0.47 -> + EHP few-shot 0.31/0.51 -> + NHP few-shot 0.33/0.55 -> + NTP few-shot (final NFH) 0.39/0.58
MDH NHP-detection successful rate: >95% across SafeBench, QuestionSet, JailbreakStudy, BeaverTails, with <10% requiring human review (Table 16)
MDH jailbreak-response detection: >95% detection rate, <10% human review across four response sets (Table 17)

Complemented Rejection Rate (CRR) on GPT-4o: SafeBench 0.44 -> RTA-SafeBench 0.17; JailbreakStudy 0.40 -> RTA-JailbreakStudy 0.14; BeaverTails 0.50 -> RTA-BeaverTails 0.10; MaliciousEducator stays at 0.00 pre- and post-cleaning (Table 3)
For GPT-4.1, o3, o4-Mini, CRR falls to 2-5% after excluding Adult Content prompts
RTA suite size: 1,155 prompts across 14 malicious categories
baselines_compared: TAP (Tree of Attacks with Pruning, black-box template)
D-Attack (paper's own 4-step baseline, system and developer role variants, without the fabricated-CoT user template)
